original_abstract: The challenges inherent in serving as a minister of God are persistent and profound, often leading many to withdraw or struggle to meet the expectations of their calling. For those specifically called to divine service, these pressures can become overwhelming. This article employs a qualitative method to perform an exegetical exposition of 2 Timothy 1:1-18, arguing for the necessity of a "theology of perseverance" for those in ministry. By focusing on Paul’s exhortations to Timothy, the research identifies perseverance as a foundational element of pastoral character. The study seeks to understand Timothy’s socio-psychological burdens and the volatile environment of the Ephesian church to better illuminate the theological messages intended for those facing ministry fatigue. Findings indicate that long-term ministerial success is not merely a product of human willpower, but a divine empowerment through the Holy Spirit and the imitation of Christ’s own endurance. The results highlight the specific challenges faced by Timothy—such as social isolation and emotional exhaustion—and offer a framework for modern practitioners to integrate these biblical principles into contemporary church leadership. Ultimately, this research enriches both the theological and practical understanding of Christian service, encouraging a resilient commitment to the gospel in the face of modern ministerial trials.
humanized_abstract: Life as a minister of God is a tough calling. It is even tougher for those who have been called to the divine service. Using qualitative methodology to do an exegetical analysis of 2 Timothy 1:1-18, this article seeks to establish the necessity of a “theology of perseverance” for ministers. By examining the exhortations that Paul gives to Timothy in 2 Timothy 1:1-18, this research revealed that perseverance is a fundamental characteristic of a minister. It then seeks to offer some insights into Timothy’s socio-psychological burden and the volatile situation in the church at Ephesus in order to shed light on the intended message of Scripture for those who are experiencing what we may term “ministry fatigue.” Based on the biblical text, this research concludes that long term success in a ministerial calling is not a matter of human willpower but of divine empowerment through the Holy Spirit as well as our imitation of the perseverance of Christ. It also highlights Timothy’s unique challenges such as alienation and emotional exhaustion, and aims to enable contemporary church leaders in the church to translate the biblical concept of perseverance into practical application. The research aims to contribute to a more comprehensive theology of Christian ministry which will enhance both the scriptural and practical aspects of the service of the gospel for today’s church leaders.
